title: Optimal Sequential Selling Mechanism and Deal Protections in Mergers and Acquisitions
abstract: We study the dynamic profit-maximizing selling mechanism in a merger and acquisitions (M&A) environment with costly bidder entry and without entry fees. Depending on the parameters, the optimal mechanism is implemented by a standard auction or by a two-stage procedure with exclusive offers to one bidder followed by an auction potentially favoring that bidder. The optimal mechanism may involve common deal protections like termination fees, asset lockups, or stock option lockups. Our proposed procedures resemble sales of targets filing Chapter 11 bankruptcy or M&A involving public targets, and they shed light on how to use deal protections in practice.

article.introduction:
Selling procedures are an importantyet understudied aspect of mergers and acquisitions (M&A). Conventional wisdom suggests that to maximize the profit from a sale, the target should use standard auctions, where the target invites all potential bidders to a bidding contest at the same time. In practice, however, targets also conduct sequential negotiations, where bidders are contacted one after another. These sequential mechanisms are also oftenasymmetric, favoring one bidder by means ofdeal protections.
  Deal protections are widely adopted in M&A. Common deal protection provisions include termination fees, asset lockups, and stock option lockups. A termination fee is an expense payable to a losing bidder. An asset lockup occurs when a losing bidder is given the option of buying certain assets of the target company, typically at a discounted price. A stock option lockup gives a losing bidder the right to buy a specified number of shares at a specified strike price. Restrepo and Subramanian (2017) find that termination fees are included in 98% of deals announced between 2003 and 2015 in which the target is a U.S. public company, with sizes of about 3% to 4% of deal value. While asset lockups and stock option lockups are historically less common than termination fees, they have become increasingly popular in the past decade. The use of termination fees is also a standard practice in the sale of bankrupt companies that file for Chapter 11 bankruptcy, with a so-called “stalking-horse bidder” granted terminating fee protection for making an initial bid that serves as a floor price for an ensuing auction (Gilson, Hotchkiss, and Osborn (2016)).
  Despite being common, the asymmetric treatment of bidders with deal protections remains controversial in courts and among scholars. The question is whether such treatment is consistent with a target management's fiduciary duty to maximize profit from an M&A sale. The courts have upheld the use of deal protections in some cases and ruled against them in others. Some legal and economics scholars maintain that deal protections reduce the target's profit by deterring nonrecipient bidders from entering the bidding contest (Kahan and Klausner (1996)). Others argue that such provisions benefit target shareholders by encouraging a recipient bidder to participate through compensation for initiating a bid (Berkovitch, Bradley, and Khanna (1989), Berkovitch and Khanna (1990)), by serving as a commitment device on the part of the target (Fraidin and Hanson (1994), Povel and Singh (2006)), or both (Che and Lewis (2007)).
  Addressing this controversy requires a comprehensive theoretical framework, yet formal theoretical studies on deal protections are limited. In this paper, we build a dynamic mechanism design framework to systematically analyze the optimal selling approach, which may involve deal protections. Our results shed light on how to use deal protections optimally in practice. Specifically, we design an optimal dynamic mechanism for a target facing two potential bidders. Each bidder lacks prior information about the target's value, unless it incurs a cost to acquire a private signal and enter the mechanism. The target can choose the number of stages, order of invitation to entry, information disclosed to bidders, as well as allocations and transfers at the end of the game, subject to the key restriction that a bidder can exit after entry without paying anything to the target. This restriction effectively rules out entry fees payable to the target, which is consistent with the rarity of entry fees in M&A. We focus on deterministic mechanisms and pure-strategy implementable outcomes. The space of deterministic dynamic mechanisms is too large to be tractable, but we derive a dynamic version of the revelation principle to reduce it. As a result, we can focus on a family of two-stage direct mechanisms, which we refer to as “canonical mechanisms,” in which the target reveals no more information than the entry recommendations made to the bidders. In addition, each bidder follows the recommendations, truthfully reports its signal, and does not exit once entered.
  Depending on the level of the entry cost, the optimal canonical mechanism takes different forms. When the entry cost is low, both bidders enter simultaneously and the target is sold to the bidder with the higher valuation. When the entry cost is high, only one bidder enters and this single bidder acquires the target firm. When the entry cost is an intermediate value, the optimal mechanism isdynamicin the sense that one bidder enters and then the second bidder's entry depends on the initial bidder's signal of its valuation. If the signal is above a cutoff, the second bidder does not enter and the target is sold to the initial bidder. If the signal is below the cutoff, the second bidder enters but the allocation rule isbiasedin favor of the initial bidder, that is, the initial bidder may win despite a valuation lower than that of the second bidder. Overall, the optimal canonical mechanism prescribes that the initial bidder is more likely than the second bidder to acquire the target ex ante, reflectingfavoritismtoward the initial bidder. Moreover, the dynamic nature of the mechanism results ininteriminformation revelation, in the sense that the target's entry recommendation for the second bidder is informative about the first bidder's signal.
  When comparing the profit-maximizing optimal canonical mechanism to the socially efficient mechanism, two potential distortions may arise. First, the entry of a second bidder is excessive in the optimal mechanism because the target does not fully internalize bidders' entry costs. Second, conditional on both bidders entering, the allocation rule could be distorted in favor of the initial bidder. Such asymmetric treatment is needed to compensate the initial bidder, not only for its entry, but also for the use of its information to determine the entry of a second bidder. While money is always available to transfer utility, the allocation distortion turns out to be a cheaper way to compensate the initial bidder. The nature of the distortion is different from the “handicapped” auction in Myerson (1981) because, in our model, the bidders have a symmetric signal distribution but the initial bidder has an endogenous informational disadvantage at the time of entry.
  The optimal canonical mechanism is too stylized to use in practice. We therefore propose several realistic selling procedures that implement the outcome of the optimal canonical mechanism, which varies with the entry cost. When the entry cost is low, the maximum target profit is achieved by a standard ascending auction with the reserve price equal to the target's stand-alone value. When the entry cost is high, the implementation is an exclusive posted price to one bidder, which, if accepted, is then open for a second bidder to challenge in the form of a standard ascending auction. In equilibrium, the second bidder is deterred from entering the auction, and the selling procedure is effectively a take-it-or-leave-it offer to the first bidder.
  Intermediate entry costs present a more complex case in which implementation is a two-stage procedure involving a distorted allocation rule. In the first stage, an initial bidder receives an exclusivemenuconsisting of a high and a low initial bid, and its choice is publicly announced. If the high initial bid is chosen, the bid is then open for a second bidder to challenge in a standard ascending auction; however, the auction does not occur in equilibrium. If instead the low initial bid is chosen, the second bidder enters and learns its signal. If its signal is too low, the second bidder drops out immediately because it cannot beat the first bidder's low initial bid. Otherwise, the two bidders compete in anasymmetricascending auction that favors the first bidder.
  For the exact form of the asymmetric auction, we propose several versions that all implement the same outcome. In the first version, the low initial bid serves as the reserve price and the initial bidder is entitled to a termination fee if it eventually loses. If the second bidder wins, it must pay the bid plus the termination fee. Importantly, the termination fee is not constant—itincreaseswith the ongoing bid in the auction so as to encourage the initial bidder to bid more aggressively. As a result, the equilibrium allocation favors the initial bidder. The termination fee does not serve simply as entry cost reimbursement; rather, it biases the allocation toward the initial bidder. More importantly, it does not deter the entry of the second bidder as it arises only when both bidders enter. In addition, it is paid in equilibrium with positive probability. Indeed, if the target intends to deter the entry of a second bidder, a high initial bid alone—without termination fee—would suffice. This version resembles and lends theoretical support to so-called “stalking-horse auctions” of bankruptcy sales of firms that file Chapter 11 bankruptcy, where in about 38% of such cases, a new bidder defeats the initial bidder in the asymmetric auction, resulting in the payment of the termination fee (Gilson, Hotchkiss, and Osborn (2016)). While in practice a stalking-horse auction usually features a fixed termination fee, our theory suggests that a price-dependent termination fee can further enhance the seller's profit.
  The second version of asymmetric auction features a bidding rule biased toward the initial bidder, such that the second bidder wins only if its bid is higher than that of the initial bidder by a (varying) wedge. By bending the bidding rule in favor of the initial bidder, the target subsidizes the bidder's informational disadvantage at the time of entry. In practice, the target may find it difficult to commit to the biased bidding rule because, the second (unfavored) bidder, if losing to the first despite a higher valuation, would challenge the outcome by submitting an unsolicited topping bid. However, the target can finalize the deal with the initial bidder by granting a termination fee sufficiently high that the losing bidder cannot afford to challenge. As in the first version, the termination fee is not used to deter the entry of the second bidder, as it emerges only when both bidders enter. However, the nature of the termination fee is completely different. Here the termination fee is acommitment deviceto support a biased auction and therefore is never paid in equilibrium. This version is similar to the prevalent selling mechanisms in the M&A of public target firms in the United States, where termination fees, while commonly used, are paid less than 2% of the time according to data from FactSet.
  Finally, we introduce versions of asymmetric auctions that involve asset lockups or stock option lockups. We show that they implement the maximum target profit in a manner similar to that of termination fees.
  We discuss the effects of relaxing certain assumptions in two extensions. The first extension drops the assumption of common knowledge of gains from trade between bidders and the target, as well as the independence of signals between the two bidders. With an alternative distribution of bidder signals, we show that most qualitative results from the main model hold. The second extension studies asymmetric bidders in terms of entry cost or signal distribution. Due to lack of tractability, we use numerical examples to show that it is profitable to first invite the bidder with a lower entry cost or a better signal distribution. However, since the second bidder then has a higher cost or a worse signal distribution, it demands better treatment in order to participate than in the case of homogeneous bidders. This effect may or may not reverse the distortion that favors the first entrant, depending on whether the difference in cost or distribution is drastic.
  Our study contributes to the sizable literature on auction and mechanism design with costly entry or information acquisition, in particular the thread that studies environments in which buyers possess no private information without paying a cost (McAfee and McMillan (1987), Bergemann and Välimäki (2002), Ye (2004), Compte and Jehiel (2007), Bulow and Klemperer (2009), Crémer, Spiegel, and Zheng (2009), Jehiel and Lamy (2015)). Among them, Crémer, Spiegel, and Zheng (2009) show that the profit-maximizing mechanism is achieved by an efficient mechanism that uses entry fees to extract all buyers' surpluses. Bulow and Klemperer (2009) investigate a sequential bidding game with preemptive bidding and an unbiased auction rule, and demonstrate that auctions with unrestricted entry typically generate more profit than the sequential game. We contribute to the literature by studying a general dynamic mechanism design problem with realistic features from the M&A environment. Specifically, we exclude entry fees due to the difficulty of implementing them in practice, and we consider various commitment devices available in practice, such as standstill agreements (to restrict preemptive bidding) and deal protections (to implement a biased auction rule). Under such a setting, the optimal mechanism is both inefficient and sequential.
  The paper is also related to the literature on costly entry (or costly information acquisition) that assumes that buyers know their valuation or a signal of it before paying the information acquisition cost (Samuelson (1985), Ye (2007), Shi (2012), Quint and Hendricks (2018), Lu, Ye, and Feng (2021), Bernhardt, Liu, and Sogo (2020)). Among them, Shi (2012) finds that the optimal one-shot mechanism may take the form of asymmetric standard auctions with different reserve prices for different bidders. Bernhardt, Liu, and Sogo (2020) show that the optimal static mechanism can take the form of a second-price auction with entry cost reimbursement and asymmetric reserve prices. Similar to these two papers, our optimal mechanism is also asymmetric. However, our optimization problem is dynamic in nature. As a result, the optimal dynamic mechanism features a two-stage procedure that allows for (i) later entry to depend on the signal of the earlier entrant, and (ii) information revelation in between entries. Lu, Ye, and Feng (2021) derive the optimal dynamic “shortlisting” mechanism, where later entry depends on earlier entrants' signals, but the implementation of their mechanism requires entry fees, which are not allowed in our setting of M&A.
  This paper belongs to the broad literature on dynamic mechanism design. Unlike papers that study the environment in which agents costlessly obtain private signals that evolve over time (Pavan, Segal, and Toikka (2014), Sugaya and Wolitzky (2021)), we consider an environment in which learning signals is costly (similar to Gershkov and Szentes (2009)).
  The paper also relates to the literature on deal protections. Among legal scholars, Ayres (1990) claim that lockups do not affect the allocation efficiency of takeovers once the bidding contest starts with “nonforeclosing bids,” while Coates and Subramanian (2000) object to this argument by focusing on certain buy-side frictions. Kahan and Klausner (1996) argue that deal protection reduces the target's profit by deterring nonrecipient bidders from entering the bidding contest. Others claim that such provisions actually benefit target shareholders because they encourage a recipient bidder to participate by compensating the bidder for initiating a bid (Berkovitch, Bradley, and Khanna (1989), Berkovitch and Khanna (1990)), or they serve as a commitment device for the target (Fraidin and Hanson (1994)). Economists have examined the role of deal protections empirically (Burch (2001), Bates and Lemmon (2003), Officer (2003), Boone and Mulherin (2007), Gilson, Hotchkiss, and Osborn (2016) ). Burch (2001) find that stock option lockups discourage competition but improve the bargaining power of target management and enhance target returns. Boone and Mulherin (2007) show that when private bidding stage competition is taken into account, termination provisions are positively related to takeover competition. Gilson, Hotchkiss, and Osborn (2016) show that despite the presence of termination fees, post-initial-bid competition is prevalent in sales of bankrupt targets.
  However, formal theoretical works on deal protections are limited. Che and Lewis (2007) study the target's optimal choice of deal protections in a sequential bidding game while imposing exogenous assumptions on the game structure, such as a fixed termination fee schedule and a low initial bid. They demonstrate that termination fees do not distort allocation, while stock option lockups do. Betton, Eckbo, and Thorburn (2009) consider an exogenously given two-stage selling procedure in which termination fees are also nondistortionary. We contribute to the literature by being among the first to study deal protections within a general dynamic mechanism design framework. We find that instead of being a constant, the optimal termination fees may vary with the initial bidder's bid in the auction, effectively distorting the allocation rule. Our paper sheds light on how to use deal protections optimally in practice.
  The paper also belongs to the broader literature on M&A selling procedures and dynamics (Fishman (1988), Povel and Singh (2006), Gorbenko and Malenko (2018), Vasu (2018), Cong (2020), Gorbenko and Malenko (2021)). Closely related are Povel and Singh (2006), who also derive a two-stage optimal mechanism consisting of an exclusive offer stage and an asymmetric auction stage. Our model differs in two respects. The first is the driving force for asymmetry. Their model features costless entry and asymmetrically informed bidders, such that the optimal mechanism, which is static, favors the better-informed bidder if her valuation is high, and otherwise favors the other bidder. The asymmetric treatment serves to facilitate rent extraction from the more informed bidder. In contrast, our model derives an optimal mechanism, which is dynamic, that always distorts the allocation in favor of the initial bidder despite the ex ante symmetric bidders. The favoritism economizes on entry cost and compensates the initial bidder for its information externality. Our model also differs in the nature of deal protections. Povel and Singh (2006) argue informally that deal protections, which are never paid in equilibrium, serve as commitment devices to support the asymmetric auction. We propose a similar implementation in SectionIV.B.2, but we further provide the lower bound of such termination fees to support the biased auction. Moreover, we introduce another implementation in SectionIV.B.1involving an asymmetric auction with termination fees, with the termination fees often paid in equilibrium. Such an implementation resembles the sales of distressed targets in bankruptcy courts.
  In another related work, Gorbenko and Malenko (2021) study a game of M&A deal initiation, in which all players, including the target and two privately informed bidders, can potentially initiate a first-price sealed-bid auction with no reserve price. In equilibrium, a bidder's indication of interest reveals that she is optimistic about the asset. The authors find that when the bidders' values have a substantial common component, this information revelation discourages bidders from initiating the sale process. Though different in terms of both the research questions and methodology, our model shares with theirs the following features: (i) ex ante identical bidders can become endogenously asymmetric, and (ii) there is a disadvantage of being the first bidder due to the ex interim information revelation to the second bidder. They also argue that toeholds on the part of the initiating bidder, which serves as a distorted allocation rule in favor of the initiating bidder, could encourage initiation. This argument is related to our result that a distorted allocation rule (such as a price-varying termination fee, a biased bidding rule, or a stock option lockup) is optimally chosen to encourage the initial bidder to enter. However, our paper studies the question of deal protection, instead of deal initiation. In addition, we adopt a dynamic mechanism design framework with costly entry, instead of a game-theoretical framework with a specific auction format and no entry cost.
  The rest of the paper is organized as follows. SectionIdescribes the model. SectionIIsimplifies the profit-maximizing problem by deriving a dynamic revelation principle and then using first-order approach. It also solves the first-best as a benchmark. SectionIIIanalyzes the key trade-offs and solves the optimal selling mechanism. SectionIVprovides several commonly observed selling procedures that implement the optimal mechanism. SectionVextends the model in two directions. SectionVIconcludes.
article.I. The Model:
There are three players in the market: a target firm and two potential bidders labeledi=1,2$i=1,2$. All players are risk-neutral. The target's stand-alone value and the bidders' outside options are normalized to zero.
  Bidders do not observe the signals in the beginning. However, if invited by the target, Bidderihas the option to incur a costc>0$c>0$toenterthe selling procedure and privately learn its own signalsi$s_i$. Note that this cost is not an entry fee collected by the target and hence does not enter the target's profit. We assume thatc<μ$c<\mu$, that is, the entry cost is not prohibitively high; otherwise, the optimal mechanism entails no sale. If a bidder is never invited or chooses not to enter after being invited, this bidder stays out of the selling procedure and receives its outside option. The entry of a bidder is observable to the target but not to the other bidder, unless the target discloses this information. The entry cost is motivated by the fact that a potential bidder incurs substantial costs to acquire information and prepare a bid. Such costs consist of fees paid to professionals such as financial advisors, lawyers, and accountants, as well as the opportunity costs of a manager's time and effort.
  The target commits to adeterministic dynamic mechanismwith a finite number of stages. At the beginning of each stagen, the target sends both bidders some public and private messages based on the target's information set. The target then invites a subset of bidders who have yet to enter, and the invited bidders simultaneously decide whether to enter. Next, each bidder who has entered and stayed in the mechanism so far takes an action (e.g., makes a bid). The action set includes permanently exiting the mechanism, after which the bidder receives its outside option of zero, but the entry costcis sunk.4The assumption on the payoffs of an exiting bidder excludes any entry fees paid to the target, which is a major deviation from the literature of mechanism design with costly entry such as Crémer, Spiegel, and Zheng (2009) and Bernhardt, Liu, and Sogo (2020). Our decision to exclude entry fees is supported by the fact that they are difficult to implement in M&A.5After stageN, the last stage, the allocation of the target firm is determined and the monetary transfers are made. Figure1summarizes the timeline of the mechanism. The formal definition of a deterministic dynamic mechanism is provided in AppendixA. The mechanism outlined above resembles M&A procedures in practice, whereby a target typically has significant control over which bidder to contact and how the bidding contest is conducted (Boone and Mulherin (2007)).6
  The target designs a deterministic dynamic mechanism to maximize its expected profit. Each bidder maximizes its expected payoff. The target and the bidders do not discount between stages. We adopt perfect Bayesian equilibrium as the solution concept.
article.II. Preliminary Analysis:
As in the traditional mechanism design literature, the first step of optimization is to apply the revelation principle and to optimize within a certain class of simpler mechanisms. However, since the mechanism design problem is dynamic, the standard argument of the static revelation principle does not apply. We therefore need to derive a particular version of the revelation principle.
  In this section, we show that it is without loss of generality to focus on a subset of deterministic dynamic mechanisms. To do so, we start by definingdirect mechanismsandcanonical mechanisms.

  Definition 2. (Canonical Mechanism)Acanonical mechanismis a direct mechanism where both bidders find it optimal to follow the entry recommendations (obedient), do not exit after entry (individually rational), and report truthfully (incentive-compatible).
  
  Note that in a canonical mechanism, the target reveals no information to a bidder other than the binary entry recommendations. Such obfuscation relaxes bidders' obedience, individual rationality (IR), and truthful-reporting constraints, making them easier to satisfy. Proposition1introduces a dynamic version of the revelation principle, showing that a focus on canonical mechanisms is sufficient.
  Proposition 1. (Dynamic Revelation Principle)Given any mapping from signal profiles to allocation, transfer, and entry outcomes implementable in a pure-strategy perfect Bayesian equilibrium of a deterministic dynamic mechanism, there exists a canonical mechanism that implements the same mapping.7
  
  The revelation principle differs from those in the dynamic mechanism design literature (Pavan, Segal, and Toikka (2014)) that features costless entry and evolving private signals. Such a literature relies on the dynamic revelation principle introduced in Myerson (1986) and formalized in Sugaya and Wolitzky (2021), where the designer contacts the agents simultaneously in each stage while repeating this process in multistage games. Because signals in our model are costly to obtain and fixed over time, our canonical mechanism contacts bidders sequentially instead. Our canonical mechanism works in a similar way as the “canonical mechanism” in Gershkov and Szentes (2009), the “sequential shortlisting” policy in Lu, Ye, and Feng (2021), and the “canonical extensive form” in Doval and Ely (2020).

article.III. Target Profit Maximization:
We now derive the optimal canonical mechanism, the one that maximizes the target's expected profit. Due to the interim IR, the target is often unable to extract all surplus, and as a result the optimal mechanism features both allocation and entry distortions compared to the first-best. In SectionIII.Awe make the observation that the target may want to distort the allocation in the event that both bidders enter. Then, in SectionIII.Bwe characterize the optimal canonical mechanism.

  Theorem 1. (Optimal Canonical Mechanism)Letc1≡α(μ−μ(1))$c_1\equiv \alpha (\mu -\mu _{(1)})$andc2≡α(μ−s̲)$c_2\equiv \alpha (\mu -\protect\underline{s})$. The optimal canonical mechanism always asks Bidder 1 to enter and sells to that bidder if Bidder 2 does not enter. Moreover:
  
  From the theorem, an immediate observation is that the optimal canonical mechanism has to bedynamicfor moderate entry costs, in the sense that Bidder 2's entry depends on Bidder 1's signal. To achieve this, the target has to reveal interim information to Bidder 2 in the form of binary entry recommendations, but any finer information (such as Bidder 1's exact signal) is unnecessary and even detrimental to the slackness of the obedience constraint.
  Theorem1implies that allocation distortion arises only for moderate entry costs (c1<c<c2$c_1<c<c_2$) and always favors Bidder 1. Specifically, when the entry costcis small (c⩽c1$c\leqslant c_1$), bidders' obedience constraints are not binding. As a result, the indirect channel of relaxing the obedience constraints does not benefit the target, and the bidder with higher virtual valuation wins. Due to the symmetric signal distributions, the allocation is not distorted. When the entry cost is large (c⩾c2$c\geqslant c_2$), Bidder 2 never enters and the allocation rule (functionj) for competing bidders is irrelevant. Whencis moderate (c1<c<c2$c_1<c<c_2$), the obedience constraints begin to bind and the target has to sweeten the pot to induce entry. However, the bidders have different information sets when called upon to enter. Bidder 1's entry decision is based on prior belief, whereas the mere fact that Bidder 2 is invited to enter implies the “good news” that Bidder 1 has a low signal. Therefore, Bidder 1's obedience constraint is tighter, demanding more favorable treatment. While the target can offer Bidder 1 a lump-sum transfer in the form of a cost reimbursement, marginally distorting the allocation turns out to be a cheaper way for the target to compensate Bidder 1. Intuitively, this is because the shadow cost of relaxing Bidder 1's obedience constraint by a lump-sum transfer is exactly one, while the shadow cost of distorting the allocation is close to zero when the distortion is small.
  The nature of the distortion is different from the “handicapped” auction in Myerson (1981). The initial bidder does not start with an exogenously weaker distribution; instead, it is endogenously less optimistic than the second bidder at the time of entry.
  In addition to allocation, the entry decision is distorted compared to first-best, in the sense that Bidder 2 enters with an excessively high probability. After all, the entry costs do not hit the target directly—they affect profit only through the obedience constraints. Whenλi<1$\lambda _i<1$, an increase in the entry cost hurts the target less than one-for-one, and therefore the target does not fully internalize the entry cost. As a result, there is excessive entry of Bidder 2 whenλ2<1$\lambda _2<1$, that is,c<c2$c<c_2$.13Corollary2formalizes the above discussion on entry.
  Corollary 2. (Excessive Entry of Second Bidder)For all entry costs, we haves∗⩾sFB$s^*\geqslant s^{{\it FB}}$. The inequality is strict whenc<c2$c<c_2$.
  
  Despite the excessive entry by Bidder 2, the target sells to Bidder 2 less often than to Bidder 1. This is because Bidder 1 always enters while Bidder 2 only enters sometimes; in addition, conditional on both bidders entering, the allocation rule may favor Bidder 1.
  Corollary 3. (Profit Maximizing: Favoritism to Initial Bidder)For all entry cost levels, the probability of selling to Bidder 1 is no lower than that of selling to Bidder 2. The inequality is strict whenc>c1$c>c_1$.
article.IV. Implementations with Deal Protection:
The optimal canonical mechanism, albeit concise, is too stylized to be practical. In this section, we propose realistic selling procedures that implement the optimal canonical mechanism and achieve maximum profit. Depending on the range of the entry cost, the implementation requires different levels of complexity and commitment power. To begin, we define the following two terms as components of various selling procedures that we introduce later.
  Definition 3. (Standard Ascending Auction)In astandard ascending auctionbetween two bidders, an ongoing pricebincreases continuously from some reserve price. Each bidder can exit at any ongoing price, as long as no one has exited so far. Whoever exits first loses the auction, and the other bidder wins and pays the price at which the losing bidder exits. A tie is broken with equal probability.
  
  
  Definition 4. (Two-Stage Procedure)Atwo-stage proceduretakes the following form:
  Stage 1 (Exclusive to Bidder 1): The target invites Bidder 1 and presents three options if it enters: (a) initiate a high bidb†$b^{\dagger }$, (b) initiate a low bidb̲$\protect\underline{b}$, and (c) exit.
  Stage 2: Bidder 1's choice is announced and then Bidder 2 is invited. If Bidder 2 does not enter, Bidder 1 wins and pays its initial bid in cases (a) and (b), otherwise the sale fails in case (c) or if Bidder 1 does not enter. If Bidder 2 enters, one of the following scenarios ensues depending on Bidder 1's choice.
  Stage 2a – if Bidder 1 chooses (a): Standard ascending auction with reserve priceb†$b^{\dagger }$.
  Stage 2b – if Bidder 1 chooses (b): Asymmetric ascending auction.
  Stage 2c – if Bidder 1 does not enter or chooses (c) after entry: Bidder 2 is offered a price of zero.
  
  Note that in the definition, the asymmetric ascending auction in Stage 2b is not yet defined. As we present various forms of implementation involving Stage 2b, the auction will appear as different versions.
  In the rest of SectionIV, we propose implementations of the optimal mechanism for various levels of entry cost.
  Consider first the implementation for low entry costs (c⩽c1$c\leqslant c_1$). The optimal canonical mechanism asks both bidders to enter, and the allocation rule between the two bidders is unbiased. Proposition3states that the maximum profit can be achieved by a standard ascending auction.
  Proposition 3. (Low Cost: Standard Ascending Auction)When0<c⩽c1$0<c\leqslant c_1$, the maximum profit of the target in the optimal canonical mechanism can be achieved in a perfect Bayesian equilibrium of a selling procedure, where bidders simultaneously decide whether to enter a standard ascending auction with zero reserve price.14
  
  Next, we consider the case of high entry costs (c2⩽c<μ$c_2\leqslant c<\mu$). The optimal canonical mechanism asks only Bidder 1 to enter. Proposition4states that the maximum profit can be implemented by a degenerate version of the two-stage procedure defined in Definition4, where in Stage 1, option (a) specifiesb†=μ−c$b^{\dagger }=\mu -c$and option (b) (i.e., initiate a low bid) is removed.
  Proposition 4. (High Cost: Two-Stage with Symmetric Auction)Supposec⩾c2$c\geqslant c_2$.
  
  
  The implementation in the proposition above is essentially static, as Bidder 2, deterred by the high bidb†$b^{\dagger }$, refuses to enter in equilibrium. In other words, the procedure is equivalent to a take-it-or-leave-it offer to Bidder 1. In addition, Bidder 2 is deterred from entry by the initial bidb†$b^{\dagger }$, even though the rule of the auction ensuing the initial bidb†$b^{\dagger }$does not favor Bidder 1.
  Despite the simple implementation for the case of high entry costs, certain commitment power from the target is implicitly required. First, if Bidder 1 initiates the bidb†$b^{\dagger }$, the target commits to hold the exact symmetric auction described in Stage 2a in the off-equilibrium event that Bidder 2 enters. Without this commitment, a sequentially rational target might solicit Bidder 2 to enter to further boost the price, by means of cost reimbursement or deal protections favoring Bidder 2. Expecting this, Bidder 1 will not choose option (a) in the first place.15One way the target can commit not to subsidize Bidder 2 upon receiving the high bidb†$b^{\dagger }$is to sign a contract with Bidder 1 that explicitly prevents the target from actively soliciting other bidders (e.g., by including a “no shop” provision). Another solution is to offer Bidder 1 a termination fee so high that, when taken into consideration, a sequentially rational target finds it unprofitable to induce Bidder 2 to enter.16
  Second, the target commits not to sell to Bidder 1 below the reserve priceb†$b^{\dagger }$. This serves to extract ex ante surplus from Bidder 1 to the extent that it is barely willing to enter. In practice, targets often reject offers that are low but far above current market share prices, indicating the common use of reserve prices.17To justify the rejection of such offers, targets frequently mention “strategic alternatives” other than an M&A with the current bidder, including searching for other bidders, or abandoning M&A altogether in favor of an initial public offering or a divestiture (Povel and Singh (2006), Liu, Mulherin, and Brown (2018)).18For M&A of distressed firms in bankruptcy courts, a target has the alternative choice of a traditional reorganization in addition to a sale. A target could also rely on the reputation of the investment bank that helps conduct the sale to commit to a reserve price.
  Third, the seller commits not to raise the reserve price of the second-stage auction once Bidder 1 has entered and agreed to initiate the bidb†$b^{\dagger }$. Without the commitment power, Bidder 1 would not enter in the first place. In practice, there are external mechanisms that prevent a target from setting an unreasonably high reserve price (e.g., the Revlon Rule of 1986, which requires that the Board of the target fulfill its fiduciary duty and maximize shareholder value in an M&A). It is understandable to the court or to target shareholders that a high reserve price can decrease competitiveness of the sale process and the likelihood of a sale. An unreasonably high reserve price therefore has a high expected likelihood and cost of litigation, and hence many targets would be reluctant to raise the reserve price too much once an initial bidder has entered.19
  Notably, the implementations for both low and high entry costs have the desirable feature that the target cannot deviate from the proposed mechanisms in a way that no bidder can detect (i.e., there are no “safe” deviations as defined in Akbarpour and Li (2020)).20As a result, in the terminology of Akbarpour and Li (2020), the mechanisms are “credible.”
  In this section, we turn to the case of moderate entry costs (c1<c<c2$c_1<c<c_2$). This is the range of entry costs wherein the optimal canonical mechanism must be sequential (Theorem1), in the sense that Bidder 2's entry depends on Bidder 1's signal. When both bidders enter, the optimal allocation is distorted in favor of Bidder 1. In SectionsIV.B.1toIV.B.3, we provide several implementations that achieve the maximum profit for the target, all of which take the form of the two-stage procedure (Definition4). All these implementations fulfill the same allocations, transfers, and target profit as those of the optimal canonical mechanism; they differ only in the format of the asymmetric ascending auction in Stage 2b, which involves commonly used deal protections such as termination fees, asset lockups, and stock option lockups.
  In this implementation, we introduce an asymmetric auction featuring price-dependent termination fees, which serves as compensation to the initial bidder to induce its entry. Such an implementation is similar to the so-called stalking-horse auctions in bankruptcy sales. We start by introducing the first version of the Stage 2b asymmetric auction.
  Definition 5. (Stage 2b.1: Price-Dependent Termination Fees)The target commits to a termination feeτ(·)$\tau (\cdot )$as a function of an ongoing bid. First, Bidder 2 decides whether to drop out at the initial ongoing bidb̲$\protect\underline{b}$, and if so, Bidder 1 wins at priceb̲$\protect\underline{b}$. Otherwise, an asymmetric auction starts, where the ongoing bidbcontinuously increases fromb̲$\protect\underline{b}$until at least one bidder exits. If Bidder 1 exits first atb, Bidder 2 wins, pays the targetb, and pays Bidder 1 the termination feeτ(b)$\tau (b)$.21If Bidder 2 exits first atb, Bidder 1 wins and pays the targetb.22A tie is broken with equal probability.
  
  Proposition 5. (Moderate Cost: Two Stages with Termination Fees)Supposec1<c<c2$c_1<c<c_2$, and letb†$b^{\dagger }$,b̲$\protect\underline{b}$, β1, β2, and τ be defined by (9) to (13).
  
  Figure4illustrates the equilibrium path of the implementation in Proposition5.
  Proposition5has important implications regarding the use of termination fees. First, part (ii) shows that if Bidder 1's initial bid is high (b†$b^{\dagger }$), then Bidder 2 is deterred from entering despite the absence of a termination fee in the subsequent Stage 2a. Therefore, a sufficiently high initial bid already deters Bidder 2, and a hence termination fees is unnecessary. This insight echoes that of Proposition4.
  In addition, a termination fee optimally arises in Stage 2b.1 when the initial bid is low (b̲$\protect\underline{b}$). In this case, the purpose of the termination fee is to compensate Bidder 1 for its positive externality in attracting competition from Bidder 2. Indeed, when Bidder 1's choice of low initial bid is announced, Bidder 2 infers that Bidder 1 is a weak competitor and hence is motivated to enter. Expecting to be exploited by the target this way, Bidder 1 demands sufficient compensation to enter in the first place.
  The variable termination fee is a major deviation from the existing literature on termination fees such as Che and Lewis (2007) and Betton, Eckbo, and Thorburn (2009). In a setting closely related to ours, Che and Lewis (2007) study a fixed termination fee and argue that it is not distortionary, but rather a monetary transfer from the target to the first bidder to subsidize the costly entry. Our analysis shows that a price-dependent termination fee, as opposed to a constant fee, is the optimal way to favor the initial bidder.23
  Proposition5(ii) suggests that the lack of competition after an initial bid could optimally arise as an equilibrium outcome. After a high initial bidb†$b^{\dagger }$, Bidder 2 does not enter and no competing bid emerges. After a low initial bidb̲$\protect\underline{b}$, Bidder 2 enters to acquire its signals2, yet still drops out before the auction starts if its signal is too low (s2<j(s̲)$s_2< j(\protect\underline{s})$). Nevertheless, when Bidder 2 does enter, the termination fee is paid with a positive probability (part (iv)).
  The target's commitment power is important in implementing the proposed procedure in practice. As in the case of high entry costs, this implementation has the merit of being “credible” (Akbarpour and Li (2020)), but it similarly requires that if Bidder 1 initiates the high bidb†$b^{\dagger }$, the target commits not to subsidize Bidder 2 to enter the auction, and the target commits to the reserve priceb̲$\protect\underline{b}$in Stage 2b.24
  Besides the above, the implementation requires additional commitment power. First, after Bidder 1 enters and accepts the low initial bidb̲$\protect\underline{b}$, the target must adhere to the promised termination fee schedule. Without such commitment, the target could renege on the promise and could instead solicit Bidder 2 to enter by running a standard auction without termination fees. Expecting this holdup problem, Bidder 1 would not have entered in the first place. In practice, the use of termination fees is ubiquitous in M&A deals,25indicating that targets commit to their promises. Targets may choose to commit by relying on the reputations of investment banks that repeatedly arrange sales in the M&A market. A bidder can also condition its initial bid on a contracted termination fee schedule, forcing the target to fulfill its commitment.
  Second, Bidder 1 cannot choose its initial bid outside the given menu ofb†$b^{\dagger }$andb̲$\protect\underline{b}$. For example, Bidder 1 cannot preempt by publicly announcing an offer slightly higher thanb̲$\protect\underline{b}$, as a signal to deter Bidder 2 from entering. The ability of the target to prevent such behavior is essential in inducing sufficient competition. In practice, a target often prevents such preemptive bidding by signing a “standstill” agreement with the first bidder, according to which the bidder cannot submit public bids without the target's permission.26
  This implementation resembles court-supervised sales of bankrupt firms that file for Chapter 11. The major selling mechanism for bankrupt targets is the so-called stalking-horse auction. In the first stage, a stalking-horse bidder submits an initial bid in exchange for a termination fee. The target then signs a contract with that bidder that specifies the bid and termination fee, and announces the contract publicly. In the second stage, an asymmetric auction between the stalking-horse bidder and additional bidders is conducted, where the initial bid serves as a floor price, and the termination fee potentially twists the bidding rule in favor of the stalking-horse bidder. Gilson, Hotchkiss, and Osborn (2016) identify stalking-horse auctions as the dominant mechanism among bankruptcy sales under Chapter 11, used in 84.3% of “363 sales” and 67.2% of sales within reorganization plans.27Although the initial bids are protected by termination fees, competing bids that top the initial bid show up in 52% of the transactions. Conditional on the existence of competing bids, the stalking-horse bidder wins the auction only 27.2% of the time and frequently pays a significant premium to their initial offers. The final price increases over the initial bid in 56.1% of sales and increases by over 25% of the initial bid in 22.8% of sales. Based on the statistics, Gilson, Hotchkiss, and Osborn (2016) provide, termination fees are paid to the stalking-horse bidder in about52%(1−27.2%)=38%$52\%(1-27.2\%)=38\%$of cases.
  The existence of post-initial-bid competition and the frequent payment of termination fees are consistent with the predictions in Proposition5, and indicate that the use of termination fees in stalking-horse auctions may help improve a seller's profit. However, a stalking-horse auction in its current form typically features a fixed termination fee that is independent of the stalking-horse bidder's bid in the second-stage auction. Our analysis in Proposition5implies that allowing the termination fee to be contingent on the initial bidder's subsequent bids can boost the profit even further. In the following numerical example with uniform signal distribution and moderate entry cost, we illustrate the optimal canonical mechanism and its implementation in Proposition5under reasonable parameter values. The target's profit is then compared with two benchmarks: standard ascending auction and two-stage procedure with a fixed termination fee.

  As we illustrate, the implementation in SectionIV.B.1, which involves a termination fee that is frequently paid, resembles the sale of a target firm in bankruptcy court.   As in the case of SectionIV.B.1, the termination fee here is not used to deter the entry of the second bidder, as it only emerges where both bidders enter. However, the structure of the termination fee in this implementation is different. Here, Bidder 1 is entitled to termination fee protection only if it is the winner of the auction of Stage 2b, while for the implementation in SectionIV.B.1, Bidder 1 is promised a termination fee as soon as it initiates Stage 2b. As a result, the termination fee in this section serves only as a commitment device to support a biased auction and is never paid in equilibrium.32
  The selling procedure in this section is related to the common selling mechanism used in the sales of U.S. public target firms. Boone and Mulherin (2007) describe the selling process as consisting of a private and a public bidding stage. In the private bidding stage, the target invites bidders to a bidding contest and signs a merger agreement with the winner. The contract specifies the deal value and, potentially, a termination fee payable to the winner if it loses the deal later. The bidding rule of this stage can be biased especially when the bids are in securities (e.g., in the combined company's stock), in which case the fair cash value of the securities can be hard to establish in court and the target has some leeway in how to compare the bids.33Termination fees are ubiquitous in such merger agreements, showing up in 98% of cases in a sample of sales of U.S. public targets announced from 2003 to 2015 (Restrepo and Subramanian (2017)). After the public announcement of the merger agreement, the public bidding stage then takes place, during which the target may consider unsolicited bids from bidders other than the winner, if any.
  To roughly map the above selling procedures to the implementation characterized in this section, we interpret the private bidding stage as a combination of Stages 1 and 2b.2. The winner of the Stage 2b.2 asymmetric auction may be entitled to a termination fee. In addition, we interpret the public bidding stage as the off-equilibrium-path auction of Proposition7, where no competing bids shows up and the termination fee is never paid but rather only serves as a commitment device. Empirical evidence is broadly consistent with the predictions of such an implementation. Indeed, while the private bidding stage often involves significant competition,34competing bids rarely show up in the public bidding stage after a deal with a termination fee is announced. In fact, we find that competing bids appear in only 1.84% of deals with U.S. public targets announced from January 2, 2002 to May 15, 2019 using FactSet MergerMetrics data.35
  Finally, it is also worth noting that a caveat on applying this implementation to the sales of U.S. public targets is its susceptibility to agency problems. To minimize disruptions on the routine operations of a target company, a public target typically keeps the bidding contest within the private bidding stage and does not announce the deal until a winner is determined. As a result, the auction, which potentially features a biased bidding rule, is often conducted informally and behind the scenes.36This creates leeway for a self-interested target manager to abuse the biased bidding rule in favor of a bidder who offers a greater private benefit.
  We now discuss two additional forms of deal protections: asset lockups and stock option lockups. They are shown to achieve the maximum profit in a manner similar to that of termination fees.
  An asset lockup is an option given to an acquirer (bidder) to buy certain assets of the target company at a typically discounted price, in the event that the acquirer loses the deal to another bidder. Asset lockups were common in the 1980s until the Delaware courts ruled against such provisions inRevlon v. MacAndrews & ForbesandMills Acquisition Co. v. Macmillan, after which they almost disappeared in the 1990s. Since the 2008 JPMorgan-Bear Stearns deal, in which JPMorgan received an option to purchase Bear's Manhattan headquarters for $1.1 billion in the event that Bear Stearns terminated the deal, asset lockups have gradually regained popularity (Restrepo and Subramanian (2017)).37
  With an asset lockup, the implicit compensation given to the acquirer upon termination of the deal is the difference between the fair market price of the asset and the specified discount price. Therefore, it is mathematically equivalent to a termination fee as long as the fair market value of the asset is common knowledge. In this case, the analyses in SectionsIV.B.1andIV.B.2on termination fees apply to asset lockups as well. However, an asset lockup could be difficult to evaluate if there is significant uncertainty about the asset's true fair market value. This may undermine the effectiveness of an asset lockup either as compensation for the initial bidder or as a commitment device, which may explain their less frequent use as compared to termination fees.
  Unlike asset lockups, stock option lockups grant the acquirer the right to buy a specified number of shares at a specified strike price if the acquirer loses the deal to another bidder. Stock option lockups were common in the 1990s, but faded out in the 2000s.38Over the past decade, however, Restrepo and Subramanian (2017) find that stock option lockups reappeared in an equivalent form of side financing deals such as convertible debt.39
  An acquirer that loses to a new bidder will exercise the stock option and resell the shares to the new bidder at the new deal price, provided that this price is higher than the strike price. The higher the new deal price, the larger the implicit benefit from exercising the option. As a result, in an ascending auction between a bidder with a stock option lockup and a bidder without such a lockup, the former has an incentive to bid more aggressively than the latter, in the hope of boosting the deal price in case it loses. The allocation is therefore biased in favor of the lockup recipient. As with termination fees, a stock option lockup can serve as a tool to directly distort the allocation rule (SectionIV.B.1) or as a commitment device to support the outcome of a biased auction (SectionIV.B.2). We focus on the former, and replace Stage 2b.1 of SectionIV.B.1with Stage 2b.3.

  In the definition above, the strike priceb0is always below any ongoing bid, meaning that Bidder 1 exercises the option for sure in the event it loses. In effect, Bidder 1 earns a one-time benefit ofζ(b)(b−b0)$\zeta (b)(b-b_0)$by exercising the option, at the cost of Bidder 2. The stock option lockup is therefore equivalent to the price-dependent termination fee in the implementation of SectionIV.B.1, where the termination fee isτ(b)=ζ(b)(b−b0)$\tau (b)=\zeta (b)(b-b_0)$, and all results from that section apply when we letζ(b)=τ(b)b−b0$\zeta (b)=\frac{\tau (b)}{b-b_0}$. In fact, even if the stock option lock ratioζ(b)$\zeta (b)$is restricted to be a constant, the benefit from exercising the option still increases with the winning bid, and hence the stock option lockup in this case is equivalent to a termination fee that increases in price. As can be seen from the example below, the optimal mechanism can be implemented with a fixed ratio under certain types of distributions.
  Example 3. (Optimal Stock Option Lockup: Moderate Entry Cost)We use the same parameters from Example1:si$s_i$is uniformly distributed on [2,4],α=0.8$\alpha =0.8$, andc=0.4$c=0.4$. In this implementation, the high and low initial prices areb†=2.702$b^{\dagger }=2.702$andb̲=2.146$\protect\underline{b}=2.146$, respectively. The fraction of the stocksζ(b)=7%$\zeta (b)=7\%$is a constant, and the strike price is 1.945. A constant fraction implements the optimal mechanism as long as the signals follow a generalized Pareto distribution, and the uniform distribution used here belongs to this class.
article.V. Extensions:
This section relaxes the assumption that gains from trade are common knowledge between bidders and the target, as well as the independence of signals between the two bidders. With an alternative distribution of bidder signals, we show that the core qualitative results from the main part of the paper hold. Formally, fori=1,2$i=1,2$, let Bidderi's valuationui=z(αsi+(1−α)s−i)$u_i = z(\alpha s_i + (1-\alpha )s_{-i})$, wherezis an indicator of the existence of gains from trade. With probabilityp∈(0,1)$p\in (0,1)$,z=1$z=1$, in which case there are gains from trade between the target and the bidders, and the bidders' valuations have the same structure as in the main model. With the complementary probability,z=0$z=0$, under which bidders' valuations are no higher than the seller's stand-alone value, indicating no gains from trade. A bidder does not knowzunless it enters. This valuation structure is similar to Fishman (1988) and Che and Lewis (2007). In this setting, the target invites Bidder 2 if and only if (i) Bidder 1 discovers positive gains from trade (z=1$z=1$) and (ii) Bidder 1's signal is not sufficiently high (s1<s∗$s_1<s^*$). As is the case in the main model, upon being invited, Bidder 2 is encouraged by the fact that Bidder 1 is a weak competitor. Furthermore, it free rides on Bidder 1's discovery of the existence of gains from trade. This type of information externality generates an additional reason for the target to compensate Bidder 1 with a distorted allocation rule. The following proposition characterizes the optimal canonical mechanism in this case.
  Proposition 8.Letc1andc2be the same as in Theorem1. The optimal canonical mechanism always includes Bidder 1 and setsx¯1(s1)=1$\protect\overline{x}_1(s_1)=1$for alls1∈[s̲,s¯]$s_1\in [\protect\underline{s},\protect\overline{s}]$. Moreover:
  
  
  The results above are very similar to those from Theorem1, except that the entry costcis replaced bycp$\frac{c}{p}$. Intuitively, since the gains from trade exist with only probabilityp, it is as if Bidder 1's effective entry cost is multiplied by1p$\frac{1}{p}$. Bidder 2's effective entry cost is not multiplied because it free rides on Bidder 1's information about the gains from trade. The implementation of the canonical mechanism is qualitatively similar to that in the main model and is therefore omitted.
  When the bidders are asymmetric, the choice of the first bidder matters. While they may differ in various dimensions, we focus on asymmetry in entry costs and in signal distributions. Due to the complications raised by thejfunction, we are unable to derive analytical results, so we explore such settings with numerical examples.
  Example 4. (Asymmetric Costs)Letα=0.8$\alpha =0.8$. Signalsi$s_i$follows a uniform distribution on [2,4],i=1,2$i=1,2$. Also, Bidder 2's entry cost isch=0.6$c_h=0.6$.
  
  
  In this example, it is always profitable to first invite the bidder with a lower entry cost. However, because the second entrant has a higher cost, it demands better treatment than in the symmetric case in order to enter. This effect may or may not overturn the distortion toward the first entrant, depending on whether the cost difference is drastic. When the entry costs are close (case (i)), the allocation still favors the first entrant as compensation. When the entry costs differ by too much (case (ii)), the allocation favors the second entrant.
  Example 5. (Asymmetric Distributions)Letα=0.8$\alpha =0.8$andc=0.5$c=0.5$. Also, Bidder 2's signals2follows a uniform distribution on [2,4].
  
  
  Similar to Example4, numerically it is always the stronger bidder (Bidder 1) that should enter first. Also, the allocation could favor either the stronger bidder (Bidder 1) or the weaker bidder (Bidder 2) depending on how drastic the differences in distributions are. The two forces in Example4that affect which bidder to favor when both bidders enter are still present in the current setting, namely, (i) Bidder 1 deserves some favoritism as the first entrant, and (ii) Bidder 2 also demands better treatment to justify its entry as a weak competitor. The latter force becomes stronger relative to the former when the differences in distribution are more drastic. In addition, with different distributions of signals, the target may favor the weaker bidder for another reason: to discriminate against the stronger bidder in order to extract rents (Myerson (1981)). This effect is also more pronounced as the differences in distributions increase. The three forces above jointly determine which bidder to favor when both bidders enter.
article.VI. Conclusion:
We contribute to the literature by being among the first to construct realistic profit-maximizing M&A selling mechanisms that involve deal protections, using a general dynamic mechanism design framework with costly bidder entry and without entry fees. We show that the optimal dynamic mechanism may take the form of a two-stage procedure, where later entry depends on the signal of earlier entrants, and information is revealed in between entries. Compared to the socially efficient mechanism, the profit-maximizing mechanism could feature both excessive entry by the later entrant and allocation distortion in favor of the initial entrant. The allocation distortion serves to compensate the initial entrant for making a bid and attracting competition from the other bidder. Depending on the parameter ranges, the optimal mechanism is implemented by realistic selling procedures, such as a standard auction, or a two-stage procedure with exclusive offers made to one bidder followed by an asymmetric auction potentially favoring that bidder. These implementations may involve common deal protections such as termination fees, asset lockups, and stock option lockups. We show that the qualitative results in the main model extend to a setting with an alternative valuation structure and to a setting with ex ante heterogeneous bidders.
  Our proposed procedures resemble sales of targets filing for Chapter 11 bankruptcy or M&A involving public targets, and they shed light on how to optimally use deal protections in practice.
